description: Comprehensive Indian wedding catering menu dataset for destination weddings in Mexico. Covers 8 events across a multi-day celebration with vegetarian-heavy options, Jain-friendly alternatives, and Indian-Mexican fusion touches where appropriate. Designed for professional catering planning with sourcing notes for Mexico.

events.idealCuisineApproach: Indian comfort food with local Mexican touches
events.notes: First gathering — keep it approachable. Guests arriving from travel, so include familiar comfort items alongside fusion surprises. This sets the tone for the wedding weekend.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Served in small shot glasses on arrival. Mexican mangoes (Manila/Ataulfo) work beautifully here.
Jaljeera powder needs to be imported or prepared from scratch. Cumin and mint base is sourced locally.
Rooh Afza or rose syrup must be imported. Basil seeds (sabja) available at specialty stores.

starters.notes: Always a crowd-pleaser. Paneer can be made fresh or sourced from Indian stores in major Mexican cities. Can be made in tandoor or oven.
Spinach and pea patties. All ingredients easily available in Mexico.
Perfect fusion starter using Mexican corn. Served with green chutney.

starters.itemName: Vegetable Spring Rolls with Tamarind Dip
starters.notes: Jain version uses cabbage, carrots, and beans — no onion or garlic. Tamarind easily sourced in Mexico.

chaatAndStreetFood.notes: Puris must be imported or made on-site. Jain version uses no onion. Huge crowd favorite — budget for high consumption.
Puffed rice and sev need to be imported. Jain version omits onion.

mains.notes: Universally loved. Tomato and cream base easy to prepare anywhere.
Chickpeas widely available in Mexico. Chole masala powder should be imported for authentic taste.
Fusion twist — Mexican kidney beans prepared in Indian rajma style with local chiles. Perfect for the welcome night theme.

dals.notes: Urad dal and kidney beans — urad dal must be imported. Needs slow cooking for best results.

paneerDishes.notes: Bell peppers and tomatoes easily available. Paneer should be fresh-made for best quality.

vegetableDishes.notes: Potatoes and cauliflower widely available. Comfort food staple.
Smoky roasted eggplant dish. Eggplants excellent quality in Mexico.

rice.notes: Basmati rice must be imported for authentic flavor. Local long-grain rice is an acceptable substitute.
Mixed vegetables with basmati. Aromatic and colorful.

breads.notes: Requires tandoor setup. All-purpose flour and butter easily available.
Fresh garlic abundantly available in Mexico.
Whole wheat flour available. Jain-friendly — no onion or garlic.

accompaniments.notes: Yogurt easily available. Boondi may need to be imported.
Best imported from India. Making from scratch is time-consuming. Jain versions available without garlic.
Fresh cilantro and mint abundantly available in Mexico.
Tamarind is a common Mexican ingredient. Easy to prepare fresh.

desserts.notes: Khoya/mawa must be imported or made from scratch by reducing milk. Instant mix can be imported as backup.
Signature fusion dessert for welcome night. Churros are local; rabri made by reducing sweetened milk with cardamom and saffron.
Mexican mangoes are excellent for mango kulfi. Pistachios may be pricier but available.

beverages.notes: Indian tea leaves (CTC) must be imported. Spices like cardamom and ginger available locally. Essential — never skip this.
Limes and mint are staples in Mexico. Perfect for warm weather.
Mexican limes are superior to Indian nimbu — excellent quality here.

liveStations.notes: Sev, papdi, puri must be imported. Station serves pani puri, bhel puri, sev puri, dahi puri. Jain options available without onion. Needs a dedicated counter attendant.
Corn tortillas filled with paneer tikka, chole, or tandoori vegetables. Topped with green chutney and pickled onions. A highlight fusion station for the welcome event.
Requires transporting or renting a tandoor oven. Live naan, roti, paneer tikka, and tandoori vegetables. Dramatic visual appeal.

events.idealCuisineApproach: Chaat-focused, colorful drinks, light and fun
events.notes: Guests have mehndi on their hands — food must be easy to eat without utensils or served in small portions. Focus on finger foods and chaat. Bright, colorful presentation to match the festive vibe.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Raw green mangoes seasonally available in Mexico. Refreshing and tangy.
Requires specific spice mix — fennel seeds, poppy seeds, rose petals. Best to import thandai masala. Almonds and milk locally available.
Mexican Ataulfo mangoes make exceptional shakes. Sweet and creamy.
Kokum syrup must be imported. Unique deep purple color adds to mehndi theme. Digestive properties.

starters.notes: Mini cocktail-size samosas — easy to eat with mehndi on hands. Potato and pea filling.
Kachori shells can be pre-made and shipped. Filled with moong dal, yogurt, and chutneys.
Small wraps easy to hold with mehndi hands. Paneer and bell peppers in paratha wrap.

chaatAndStreetFood.notes: Puris and sev imported. Assembled to order. Jain version without onion.
Bite-sized — perfect for mehndi. Yogurt filling keeps it cool.
Papdi must be imported. Assembled with yogurt, chutneys, and sev.
Potato patties with yogurt and chutneys. All ingredients locally available.
Mexican corn is perfect here. Served with lime, chaat masala, and butter. Natural fusion.
Potato patties with white pea curry. Hearty street food option.

lightSnacks.notes: Roasted peanuts with curry leaves and spices. Quick snack between mehndi sessions. Peanuts abundantly available.
Must be imported. Thin crispy flatbreads — easy finger food. Popular with Gujarati and Jain guests.
Gram flour batter needs specific fermentation. Instant mix can be imported. Light and spongy.

accompaniments.notes: Fresh herbs and tamarind available locally. Essential for chaat stations.

desserts.notes: Made fresh at live station — dramatic visual appeal. Saffron-colored, matches mehndi theme. All ingredients available locally.
Sweet pancakes with thickened sweetened milk. All dairy ingredients available.
Falooda sev (vermicelli) and rose syrup need importing. Ice cream and milk local. Colorful layered presentation perfect for mehndi.

beverages.notes: Yogurt-based drink. Mexican yogurt works well. Served chilled with cardamom.
Rose syrup (Rooh Afza) must be imported. Beautiful pink color matches mehndi aesthetic.
Mexican tropical fruits (guava, papaya, mango) with Indian spice twist — chaat masala rim, black salt.

liveStations.notes: The centerpiece of mehndi food. Pani puri, sev puri, dahi puri, bhel puri, papdi chaat all served from one station. Import all chaat components.
Live jalebi frying is a visual spectacle. Batter fermented overnight. Saffron sugar syrup. Great Instagram moment.

events.idealCuisineApproach: Light daytime food, fresh drinks, easy cleanup. Nothing heavy or greasy — turmeric is already messy enough.
events.notes: Haldi is messy with turmeric paste. Keep food simple, light, and away from the main ceremony area. Fresh juices and light bites work best. Easy cleanup is essential.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Thematic — turmeric milk served warm or iced. Turmeric and milk locally available. Can add honey and black pepper.
Abundantly available in coastal Mexico. Served in the coconut shell for presentation. Hydrating and refreshing.
Light and refreshing. All ingredients local. Served with black salt.

starters.notes: Idli batter needs urad dal (imported) and fermentation time. Mini size for easy eating. Light and healthy.
Mexican tropical fruits with chaat masala and lime. Extremely fresh and light. Perfect for daytime outdoor event.
Gram flour rolls tempered with mustard and curry leaves. Light and delicate. Requires skilled preparation.

chaatAndStreetFood.notes: Smaller portions than at mehndi. Refreshing mint water version. Puris imported.

lightSnacks.notes: Sprouted mung dal with lime, coriander, and pomegranate. Light and nutritious. Jain-friendly.
Flattened rice must be imported. Light breakfast-style dish with peanuts, curry leaves, and turmeric.
Mixed vegetable mash with buttered bread rolls. Pav bhaji masala should be imported. Smaller portions for haldi.
Semolina (sooji/rava) may need importing for specific grade. Light savory porridge.

rice.notes: Light, tangy rice with peanuts and curry leaves. Yellow color matches haldi theme. Mexican limes work perfectly.

desserts.notes: Strained yogurt dessert with saffron. Light and cool. Mexican mangoes for mango variant. Saffron needs importing.

desserts.itemName: Fresh Fruit Platter with Chaat Masala
desserts.notes: Tropical Mexican fruits — mango, papaya, guava, pineapple — with chaat masala and lime. Light dessert option.

beverages.notes: Sugarcane available in Mexico. Served fresh with lime and ginger. Refreshing for outdoor daytime event.
Diluted yogurt with cumin, coriander, and curry leaves. Cooling and digestive. All ingredients available.
Mexican watermelons are excellent. Add a pinch of black salt and mint. Perfect for hot daytime events.

liveStations.notes: Live juicing station with tropical fruits, sugarcane, and Indian spiced lemonade options. Great visual element.

events.eventStyle: High-energy, festive evening celebration with performances
events.recommendedServiceStyle: buffet with multiple live stations and passed appetizers during performances
events.idealCuisineApproach: Festive, varied, fusion-friendly. Stronger variety and bold flavors to match the energy.
events.notes: The party night. Food needs to be available throughout the evening as guests move between performances and dining. Late-night snack options important. Live stations create energy and engagement.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Mexican margarita with Indian twist — chaat masala rim, jalapeño, and cilantro. Signature fusion cocktail moment.
Mexican guava blended with jaljeera spices. Fresh and tangy fusion drink.

starters.notes: Spicy, crispy paneer bites. Bold flavors for a party atmosphere. Curry leaves imported or grown locally.
Crispy cauliflower in spicy Indo-Chinese sauce. Always a hit at parties.
Hung curd patties, lightly fried. Melt-in-mouth texture. Elegant starter.
Mixed vegetable and paneer kebabs on skewers. Grilled on tandoor.
Mexican corn kernels deep fried with Indian spices. Easy to eat while standing. No onion/garlic version available.

chaatAndStreetFood.notes: High-volume service needed for sangeet crowds. Multiple flavored waters: mint, tamarind, mango.
Spiced potato fritter in mini buns. Excellent late-night snack option. Pav can be made locally.

lightSnacks.notes: Crunchy spiral snack — best imported pre-made. Served in bowls as table snacks during performances.

mains.notes: Grilled paneer in rich tomato-cream gravy. Crowd favorite across all events.
Paneer and potato dumplings in creamy gravy. Rich and festive. All ingredients available.
Stir-fried vegetables in spicy sauce. Jain version without onion and garlic. Colorful and vibrant.

dals.notes: Toor or moong dal — must be imported. Simple tempered lentils. Jain-friendly comfort food. Essential everyday dal.

paneerDishes.notes: Spinach and paneer — spinach available in Mexico. Rich in iron and presentation.

vegetableDishes.notes: Bell peppers stuffed with spiced paneer or potato filling. Mexican bell peppers are excellent quality.

rice.notes: Layered rice dish. Basmati rice and whole spices imported. Saffron strands needed. Dum cooking method for authentic flavor.

breads.notes: Layered flaky flatbread. Made on tawa. No onion/garlic — Jain-friendly.
Naan stuffed with melted cheese. Mexican cheeses like Oaxaca work surprisingly well. Party favorite.

accompaniments.notes: Papads must be imported. Urad and moong varieties. Served roasted or fried with masala.
Diced cucumber, tomato, onion with lemon and chaat masala. All produce locally available.

desserts.notes: Soft paneer dumplings in saffron-cardamom milk. Can be made fresh with local milk. Premium dessert option.
Grated carrot slow-cooked with milk, sugar, and ghee. Mexican carrots available but may lack the deep red color of Indian Delhi carrots. Garnish with nuts.
Assorted kulfi (malai, pista, mango) and ice cream. Kulfi can be made in advance and stored. Various toppings.

beverages.notes: Essential for late-night chai service after performances. CTC tea imported.
Mexican coffee beans make excellent filter coffee. South Indian filter and chicory blend may need importing. Strong decoction with milk.

liveStations.notes: Live dosa making — masala, plain, cheese, and mysore varieties. Batter requires urad dal (imported) and fermentation. Dramatic visual element with large tawa. Late-night favorite.
Live pasta tossing with Indian-spiced sauces — tikka masala pasta, curry pesto, etc. Appeals to younger crowd. All ingredients available in Mexico.
Betel leaves, gulkand, supari, and all paan ingredients must be imported. Meetha paan only (no tobacco). Fire paan is a showstopper. Needs a skilled paanwala.
Fresh naan, paneer tikka, tandoori mushroom, stuffed kulcha. Requires tandoor oven setup.

events.idealCuisineApproach: Traditional Indian — no fusion. Auspicious foods. Multi-course thali format preferred.
events.notes: This is the most traditional meal. Keep it authentically Indian. No fusion experiments here. Jain and vegetarian options are critical. Thali format creates a grand impression and ensures variety.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Rose syrup imported. Served chilled. Traditional and auspicious.

starters.notes: Steamed gram flour cakes. Instant mix can be imported. Light and auspicious start.
Classic preparation with no fusion twist for the ceremony meal.

mains.notes: Royal paneer in rich cashew-cream sauce. The word 'shahi' means royal — fitting for the wedding day.
Baby potatoes in rich yogurt and fennel gravy. Mild and aromatic. Traditional wedding dish.
Mixed vegetable dish with fenugreek dumplings. Some vegetables (valor, surti papdi) may be hard to source. Substitute with local equivalents.
Yogurt-based curry with gram flour fritters. Traditional wedding staple. Gram flour locally available.

dals.notes: Five-lentil dal. Lentils must be imported — urad, moong, chana, masoor, toor. Traditional and auspicious.
Rich, creamy black lentil dal. Must be slow-cooked overnight for best results.

paneerDishes.notes: Stuffed paneer slices in rich gravy. Premium dish for the wedding day. Cashew paste and cream base.

vegetableDishes.notes: Mixed seasonal vegetables in light gravy. Jain version without onion, garlic, root vegetables like potato.
Okra stuffed with spices and dry mango powder. Okra available in Mexico. Jain-friendly without onion/garlic.

rice.notes: Simple cumin-tempered basmati rice. Basmati imported.
Sweet rice with saffron, nuts, and raisins. Traditional auspicious wedding dish. Saffron imported.

breads.notes: Traditional puffed bread. Served with chole/halwa as part of the traditional wedding meal. Wheat flour available.
Gram flour and wheat flatbread with spices. Nutritious and flavorful traditional bread.

accompaniments.notes: Boondi (tiny gram flour drops) must be imported. Yogurt local.
Import branded Indian pickle — Mother's or Priya. Jain versions without garlic.

desserts.notes: Rice pudding with cardamom, saffron, and nuts. Traditional auspicious dessert. Milk and rice available locally.
Rich dessert made from moong dal, ghee, and sugar. Dal must be imported. Very labor-intensive. Premium traditional sweet.
Mandatory at an Indian wedding. Khoya imported or made from scratch.
Semolina halwa — traditionally served with puri and chole at wedding lunches. Quick to prepare. Auspicious.

beverages.notes: Spiced buttermilk to aid digestion after a heavy thali meal.
Fresh lemonade with roasted cumin and black salt. Mexican limes excellent.

events.recommendedServiceStyle: elaborate buffet with live counters and sit-down elements
events.idealCuisineApproach: Traditional Indian wedding feast — the most elaborate meal of the celebration
events.notes: The grandest meal. This is where you go all out. Maximum variety, premium ingredients, multiple live stations. Both the ceremony lunch and dinner should feel complete, but dinner is more elaborate and festive.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Traditional celebratory drink. Thandai masala imported. Served chilled with almond garnish.
Warm saffron-almond milk. Saffron and almond powder imported. Royal and auspicious drink for the wedding night.

starters.notes: Melt-in-mouth mushroom kebabs inspired by the famous Lucknowi galouti. Premium starter for premium event.
Cream-marinated paneer. Milder and more elegant than regular tikka. Cashew and cream marinade.
Baby potatoes stuffed with spiced paneer, grilled in tandoor. Hearty and impressive.

mains.notes: Rich tomato-cream paneer curry with a touch of fenugreek. Premium restaurant-style preparation.
Nine-jewel mixed vegetable korma in creamy cashew-almond sauce. Grand wedding classic with fruits and nuts.
Bell peppers stuffed with paneer-cashew filling in buttery tomato gravy. Impressive presentation.
Classic peas and paneer curry. Green peas available frozen. Comfort food that everyone enjoys.

dals.notes: The signature dal for any Indian wedding dinner. 24-hour slow cooked for best results.
Split green moong dal — lighter option alongside dal makhani. Moong dal imported. Jain-friendly.

paneerDishes.notes: Paneer cooked on a tawa with bell peppers and tomatoes. Semi-dry preparation.

vegetableDishes.notes: Fenugreek leaves with peas in cream. Fenugreek (methi) may need importing as fresh leaves are rare in Mexico. Can use dried (kasuri methi).
Baby potatoes in rich yogurt-based gravy. Slow-cooked (dum method) for deep flavor.

rice.notes: The crown jewel of rice dishes. Layered, dum-cooked. Basmati and saffron imported. Must be served with raita and mirchi ka salan.
Plain cumin rice as accompaniment to curries. Essential alongside biryani.

breads.notes: Stuffed with spiced paneer or potato. Premium bread option for the grand dinner.
Thin handkerchief bread tossed dramatically. Requires skilled chef. Great live element.

accompaniments.notes: Peanut-sesame based chili curry. Essential accompaniment with biryani. Mexican chiles work as substitute.
Fresh mint and coriander. Standard accompaniment.

desserts.notes: Premium dessert. Fresh chenna dumplings in flavored milk. Can be made with local milk.
Non-negotiable at an Indian wedding dinner. Served warm in sugar syrup.
Spongy chenna balls in sugar syrup. Made fresh from local milk. Lighter alternative to gulab jamun.
Served warm and crispy from the live station. Traditional wedding essential.
Warm carrot halwa served with a dollop of cream. Winter wedding favorite.

beverages.notes: Post-dinner chai service. Essential.
Saffron and almond green tea. Kahwa spice mix imported. Premium after-dinner option. Digestive.

liveStations.notes: Full tandoor service — naan, kulcha, paneer tikka, tandoori veg platter. The visual and aromatic centerpiece of the dinner.
Complete paan service with meetha paan, chocolate paan, fire paan. All betel leaf ingredients imported. Paanwala needed.
Assorted kulfi sticks and scoops. Flavors: malai, pista, mango, rose, kesar. Prepared in advance.

events.recommendedServiceStyle: plated dinner with passed appetizers and dessert stations
events.idealCuisineApproach: Best-of Indian cuisine presented with fine-dining elegance. Smaller portions, higher quality, beautiful plating.
events.notes: The reception is about presentation and premium quality. Every dish should look elegant. This is often the event with non-Indian guests too, so include universally appealing items. Consider plated service for the main course.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Elegant welcome drink with saffron strands visible. Mexican limes, imported saffron. Served in champagne flutes.
Rose syrup imported. Canned lychee available. Delicate, sophisticated flavor pairing.

starters.notes: Three variations of paneer tikka plated together elegantly. Showcases range of Indian flavors.

starters.itemName: Mushroom Vol-au-Vent with Tikka Masala Filling
starters.notes: French pastry shells with Indian tikka masala mushroom filling. Elegant fusion for the reception. Puff pastry available locally.
Melt-in-mouth curd kebabs garnished with pomegranate seeds. Elegant plating.
Modern Indian presentation. Broccoli available in Mexico. Appeals to health-conscious and international guests.

mains.notes: Premium version of butter paneer — plated individually with cream drizzle and kasuri methi garnish. Fine dining presentation.
Nine-gem vegetable korma. Premium plating with gold leaf garnish for reception. Rich cashew-cream sauce.
Black pepper paneer in cream sauce. Subtle and elegant flavor profile suitable for fine dining.

dals.notes: 48-hour slow-cooked for ultimate creaminess. Premium version with extra butter and cream finish. Urad dal imported.

vegetableDishes.itemName: Subz Miloni (Mixed Vegetable in Saffron Cream)
vegetableDishes.notes: Premium mixed vegetables in saffron-infused cream. Jain version without root vegetables and onion/garlic. Elegant presentation.

rice.notes: Lucknowi-style biryani with generous saffron. Individual sealed pot (handi) service for premium presentation. Basmati and saffron imported.

breads.itemName: Assorted Bread Basket (Naan, Kulcha, Roti, Paratha)
breads.notes: Elegant bread basket with variety. Served warm in cloth-lined baskets. Table service preferred over buffet.

accompaniments.notes: Fresh pineapple raita — unique and tropical. Mexican pineapples excellent. Elegant twist on traditional raita.
Modern accompaniment — dried cranberries with walnuts and Indian spices. Appeals to international palate.

desserts.notes: Signature fusion dessert — Mexican tres leches cake with rasmalai flavors (cardamom, saffron, pistachios). Show-stopping reception centerpiece.
Traditional Indian sweet — khoya-based with saffron. Khoya imported or made from scratch. Can be shaped into elegant forms.
Cashew, pistachio, and almond barfi. Nuts available but premium-priced. Can import from Indian sweet shops. Gift-worthy presentation.
Mini cheesecakes with gulab jamun pieces folded in. Cream cheese available in Mexico. Elegant individual portions.
Thickened sweetened milk with fresh Mexican tropical fruits. Light and elegant dessert option.

beverages.notes: Post-dinner chai. Served from samovar for visual appeal.
Dual coffee station — espresso for international guests, filter coffee for South Indian preference. Mexican coffee beans excellent.

liveStations.notes: Premium paan service with ice paan, chocolate paan, fire paan. Instagram-worthy presentation. All ingredients imported.
Grand display of Indian sweets — barfi, peda, ladoo, soan papdi, kaju katli. Mix of imported and freshly made. Stunning visual centerpiece.
Vintage-style cart with kulfi and premium ice cream. Live serving with toppings bar. Great photo opportunity.

events.idealCuisineApproach: Comfort food, Indian breakfast classics, and crossover brunch items. Nostalgic and filling before guests depart.
events.notes: Last meal of the wedding weekend. Keep it warm and nostalgic. Indian breakfast comfort food mixed with brunch classics. Guests are tired from the celebrations — simple, filling food is key. Include takeaway options for guests traveling.

welcomeDrinks.notes: Fresh-squeezed Mexican oranges. Simple and refreshing morning drink.
Self-serve chai from large samovar. CTC tea imported. Comfort in a cup for the goodbye morning.

chaatAndStreetFood.notes: Classic North Indian breakfast — spiced chickpeas with deep-fried bread. Hearty and satisfying.

lightSnacks.itemName: Aloo Paratha with Butter and Yogurt
lightSnacks.notes: The ultimate Indian comfort breakfast. Potato-stuffed flatbread with white butter and fresh yogurt. All ingredients available.
Light flattened rice dish. Poha flakes imported. Nostalgic comfort food.
Semolina porridge with mustard seeds and curry leaves. Light South Indian breakfast option.
Bread slices dipped in spiced gram flour batter and fried. Rainy-day comfort food. Quick to prepare.
Spicy sprouted moth bean curry with bread rolls. Farsan (crunchy toppings) imported. Bold flavors for brunch.

mains.notes: Buttery mixed vegetable mash with toasted bread rolls. Crowd-pleasing comfort food. All produce available locally.
Scrambled paneer with onion, tomato, and spices. Indian equivalent of scrambled eggs. Brunch crossover item.

dals.notes: Thin crepes made from moong dal batter. Moong dal imported. Jain-friendly with no onion/garlic. Healthy breakfast option.

vegetableDishes.notes: Simple home-style potato and cauliflower. Nostalgia comfort food for the last meal together.

breads.notes: Aloo, gobi, paneer paratha — served with butter, yogurt, and pickle. DIY paratha station is a highlight.

accompaniments.notes: Essential paratha accompaniments. White butter, fresh yogurt, and imported pickle.

desserts.notes: Warm semolina halwa — traditional breakfast dessert. Quick to prepare. Served with puri. Jain-friendly.
Strained yogurt with mango puree. Mexican mangoes perfect. Served chilled as a brunch dessert.
Small boxes of assorted sweets (kaju katli, barfi, ladoo) for guests to take home. Import from Indian sweet shops. Beautiful packaging as wedding favor.

beverages.notes: Unlimited chai service — the most important item at farewell brunch.
Strong South Indian filter coffee. Mexican coffee beans with chicory blend if possible.
Thick sweet yogurt drink. Cooling and satisfying. Local yogurt works well.

beverages.itemName: Fresh Fruit Juice Bar (Guava, Orange, Watermelon)
beverages.notes: Mexican tropical fruits freshly juiced. Multiple options. Brunch essential.

liveStations.notes: Morning dosa service — masala, plain, cheese, and rava varieties. Batter fermented overnight. With sambar and chutneys.
Live paratha making — guests choose their stuffing (aloo, paneer, gobi, methi). Served hot from the tawa with butter.

easyToSourceLocally: Paneer (can be made fresh from local milk)
All dairy — milk, cream, yogurt, butter, cheese
Fresh produce — tomatoes, onions, peppers, potatoes, cauliflower, spinach, eggplant, okra
Beans — kidney beans, chickpeas, black beans
Tropical fruits — mango (Ataulfo/Manila), papaya, guava, pineapple, watermelon
mustImportFromIndia: Basmati rice (critical — local rice is not a substitute for biryani)
Urad dal, toor dal, moong dal, chana dal, masoor dal
CTC tea leaves for masala chai
Khoya / Mawa (or plan to make from scratch — very time consuming)
Pani puri shells, papdi, sev, boondi, farsan
Thandai masala, chai masala, chaat masala, pav bhaji masala, chole masala
Betel leaves and paan ingredients (supari, gulkand, etc.)
Curry leaves (may grow locally in some regions)
Jaggery (Indian variety — though Mexican piloncillo is a close substitute)
Khakhra, chakli, and other dry snacks
Instant mixes (dhokla, idli, dosa batter premix as backup)
sourcingTips: Contact Indian grocery importers in Mexico City, Cancun, or Guadalajara — small communities exist with import channels.
Plan imports 6-8 weeks in advance to account for customs and shipping delays.
Carry critical spice mixes in checked luggage as backup.
Mexican piloncillo is an excellent substitute for Indian jaggery.
Mexican tamarind paste works perfectly for chutneys and is often fresher than imported.
Fresh paneer is best made on-site from local whole milk — superior to imported frozen paneer.
Tandoor ovens may need to be shipped or custom-built locally — coordinate with local event equipment vendors.
Hire at least 2-3 Indian chefs to travel with the catering team for authentic preparation.
Test all recipes with local ingredient substitutes at least 2 months before the event.

jainMenuNotes.description: Jain dietary guidelines for catering planning. Important for a significant portion of Indian wedding guests.
restrictions: No onion or garlic in any preparation
No root vegetables (potatoes, carrots, beets, radishes, turnips, sweet potatoes)
Strict Jain: no fermented foods, no honey
Food must be prepared fresh — not reheated from previous day
safeIngredients: All above-ground vegetables — cabbage, cauliflower, bell peppers, beans, peas, corn, tomatoes
All lentils and legumes (when sprouted, some Jains avoid)
Sugar, salt, most spices (except those derived from roots — adjust accordingly)
jainMenuNotes.cateringRecommendation: Prepare a separate Jain kitchen line with clearly labeled utensils. At minimum, offer 4-5 Jain main course options per event. Mark all buffet items clearly with Jain/non-Jain labels.
